attributes.stem:
A 45-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with an episode of confusion, sweating, palpitations and tremors. She explains that she has been experiencing similar episodes for the past two months and they have been gradually increasing in intensity. These episodes come on rapidly and are intense, often occurring in the early morning or during periods where she has not eaten for some time. After she has a sugary snack her symptoms appear to settle. She does not have any other health issues and does not use any medications. On examination, she is alert and oriented but appears anxious. Her blood pressure is 150/90 mmHg, pulse 110/min, respiratory rate 18/min and temperature 36.5°C. The physical examination is unremarkable. Laboratory investigations show the following:

  • Glucose: 2.1 mmol/L (3.9-6.1)

  • Insulin: 25 mIU/L (2-25)

  • C-peptide: 3.2 nmol/L (0.4-2.0)

  • Proinsulin: 35 pmol/L (<22)

She is given intravenous glucose infusion, which rapidly resolves her symptoms and arrangement are made for a follow-up after further investigations.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following would be the most appropriate initial investigation?
attributes.explanation:
This patient most likely has an insulinoma. She has presented with the characteristic picture of symptomatic hypoglycaemia, known as Whipple's triad:

  • neuroglycopenic symptoms

  • hypoglycaemia (plasma glucose < 2.8 mmol/L)

  • prompt relief of symptoms following the administration of glucose

The commonest causes of hypoglycaemia are inadvertent high insulin dosing or missing a meal after a regular dose of insulin. Factitious hypoglycaemia must also be considered - deliberate self-administration of insulin in a non-diabetic. However, in this case the high insulin and C-peptide levels point towards an endogenous source of excess insulin and this will most often be an insulinoma.

The best first-line imaging modality for an insulinoma in this scenario would be a contrast-enhanced CT scan of the abdomen. Insulinomas and if present, liver metastases, are typically highly vascularised lesions showing enhancement compared to normal pancreatic tissue with IV contrast administration on CT. Therefore, contrast-enhanced CT in the context of thin-slices techniques should be used in order to efficiently detect these neoplasms.

Transabdominal ultrasound is one of the first-line imaging modalities for the detection of insulinomas, but it is usually not preferred due to its low sensitivity for the localisation of insulinoma.

Abdominal X-ray has no role in the detection of insulinoma.

Endoscopic ultrasound has a very high sensitivity for the diagnosis of insulinoma but it is usually not used as a first-line modality because of its invasive nature.

Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) also has good sensitivity for the diagnosis of insulinomas, but it is usually not used as a first-line imaging modality because of the cost involved and the fact that contrast-enhanced CT scan also has very good sensitivity.
learningPoints: Contrast-enhanced CT scan is the first-line imaging modality of choice for the diagnosis of insulinoma.
references: Giannis D, Moris D, Karachaliou GS, Tsilimigras DI, Karaolanis G, Papalampros A, Felekouras E. Insulinomas: from diagnosis to treatment. A review of the literature. J BUON. 2020 May-Jun;25(3):1302-1314. PMID: 32862570.

Okabayashi T, Shima Y, Sumiyoshi T, Kozuki A, Ito S, Ogawa Y, Kobayashi M, Hanazaki K. Diagnosis and management of insulinoma. World J Gastroenterol. 2013 Feb 14;19(6):829-37. doi: 10.3748/wjg.v19.i6.829. PMID: 23430217; PMCID: PMC3574879.
